perturbation.original_text: 5. Payment of Premiums. Subject to the terms of this Agreement, the Company agrees to pay premiums on the Policy as provided under the Policy. In the event this Agreement is terminated in accordance with terms of Section 11, the Company shall not be liable for any premiums owed on the Policy after the date of termination.
perturbation.changed_text: 5. Payment of Premiums. Subject to the terms of this Agreement, the Company agrees to pay premiums on the Policy as provided under the Policy for the first five years of this agreement. In the event this Agreement is terminated in accordance with terms of Section 11, the Company shall be fully liable for any premiums owed on the Policy, regardless of termination date.
perturbation.explanation: This change introduces an in-text contradiction by stating the company pays premiums for only the first five years and then being liable for all premiums, even after termination. This contradicts the original intention of the company only being liable until termination. It creates uncertainty about premium payment obligations especially in the case of early termination or if the policy extends beyond 5 years.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 5. Payment of Premiums. Subject to the terms of this Agreement, the Company agrees to pay premiums on the Policy as provided under the Policy for the first five years of this agreement. In the event this Agreement is terminated in accordance with terms of Section 11, the Company shall be fully liable for any premiums owed on the Policy, regardless of termination date.
perturbation.justification: The changed text directly contradicts itself and the original intent. It states the company pays premiums only for the first five years but then is liable for all premiums, regardless of termination date. This creates a clear in-text contradiction within Section 5, leading to uncertainty and potential disputes over premium payment obligations. This aligns with the 'Inconsistencies - In Text Contradiction' definition where 'different sections of a contract provide conflicting deadlines, obligations, or penalties,' which applies to the premium payment obligation here.

perturbation.original_text: 10. Amendment. The Company may amend or modify this Agreement at any time, but any such amendment or modification that would adversely affect the rights of the Trust 4 under this Agreement shall not be effective without the prior written consent of the Trustees of the Trust.
perturbation.changed_text: 10. Amendment. The Company may amend or modify this Agreement at any time, and no consent from the Trustees of the Trust will be required, regardless of the adverse impact on the Trust's rights. However, the Trust also reserves the right to unilaterally amend the agreement at any point.
perturbation.explanation: This change creates an in-text contradiction. The original text allows amendments by the Company but requires Trust consent if the Trust's rights are adversely affected. The modified text first states the company can amend at any time without Trust consent, directly contradicting the original clause. Then, further contradictions are made by stating that The Trust also has the right to unilaterally amend the agreement at any point. This creates uncertainty as to who has final say in amendments and the process for doing so.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 10. Amendment. The Company may amend or modify this Agreement at any time, and no consent from the Trustees of the Trust will be required, regardless of the adverse impact on the Trust's rights. However, the Trust also reserves the right to unilaterally amend the agreement at any point.
perturbation.justification: The modified text creates a clear in-text contradiction. The original text requires the Trust's consent for amendments affecting their rights. The changed text explicitly removes this consent requirement and grants the Trust unilateral amendment rights, creating a direct conflict with the previous consent requirement. The Contradicted Text is in the same section as the Changed Text, further highlighting that this is an in-text contradiction. This adheres to the definition of 'Inconsistencies - In Text Contradiction' as it introduces conflicting obligations and uncertainties.

perturbation.original_text: 7. Company Default. In the event of a "Company Default" (as defined below), the Trust shall have the right to require the Company to cure the Company Default by notifying the Company in writing within sixty (60) days after its receipt of notice of a Company Default, or if later, within thirty (30) days after a Trustee becomes aware of the Company Default. If the Company fails to cure the Company Default within sixty (60) days after being notified by the Trust of the Company Default, the Trust shall have the right to require the Company to transfer its interest in the Policy to the Trust. The Trust may exercise this right by notifying the Company, in writing, within sixty (60) days after the Company Default occurs. Upon receipt of such notice, the Company shall immediately transfer ownership of the Policy to the Trust and the Company shall thereafter have no rights with respect to the Policy. The Trust's failure to exercise its rights under this Section 7 shall not be deemed to release the Company from any of its obligations under this Agreement, and shall not preclude the Trust from seeking other remedies with respect to the Company Default. For purposes of this Agreement, a "Company Default" shall be deemed to have occurred with respect to the Policy if the Company fails to pay a premium on the Policy as required under the terms of this Agreement within sixty (60) days after the due date for such premium, or if the Company processes or attempts to process a policy loan, or a complete or partial surrender, or a cash value withdrawal without prior written approval from the Trustees of the Trust. The Company shall notify the Trustees of the Trust within five (5) business days of any event which constitutes a Company Default.
perturbation.changed_text: 7. Company Default. In the event of a "Company Default" (as defined below), the Trust shall have the right to require the Company to cure the Company Default by notifying the Company in writing within sixty (60) days after its receipt of notice of a Company Default, or if later, within thirty (30) days after a Trustee becomes aware of the Company Default. If the Company fails to cure the Company Default within sixty (60) days after being notified by the Trust of the Company Default, the Trust shall have the right to require the Company to transfer its interest in the Policy to the Trust. However, even if the Company cures the default, the Trust still retains the right to demand immediate transfer of the policy. The Trust may exercise this right by notifying the Company, in writing, within sixty (60) days after the Company Default occurs. Upon receipt of such notice, the Company shall immediately transfer ownership of the Policy to the Trust and the Company shall thereafter have no rights with respect to the Policy. The Trust's failure to exercise its rights under this Section 7 shall not be deemed to release the Company from any of its obligations under this Agreement, and shall not preclude the Trust from seeking other remedies with respect to the Company Default. For purposes of this Agreement, a "Company Default" shall be deemed to have occurred with respect to the Policy if the Company fails to pay a premium on the Policy as required under the terms of this Agreement within sixty (60) days after the due date for such premium, or if the Company processes or attempts to process a policy loan, or a complete or partial surrender, or a cash value withdrawal without prior written approval from the Trustees of the Trust. The Company shall notify the Trustees of the Trust within five (5) business days of any event which constitutes a Company Default.
perturbation.explanation: The added sentence "However, even if the Company cures the default, the Trust still retains the right to demand immediate transfer of the policy" introduces an in-text contradiction. The original text implies that curing the default restores the Company's rights, but this sentence allows the Trust to demand the policy transfer regardless, undermining the cure provision. It creates ambiguity and makes the cure process pointless, significantly altering the intended consequence of a company default.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 7. Company Default. In the event of a "Company Default" (as defined below), the Trust shall have the right to require the Company to cure the Company Default by notifying the Company in writing within sixty (60) days after its receipt of notice of a Company Default, or if later, within thirty (30) days after a Trustee becomes aware of the Company Default. If the Company fails to cure the Company Default within sixty (60) days after being notified by the Trust of the Company Default, the Trust shall have the right to require the Company to transfer its interest in the Policy to the Trust. However, even if the Company cures the default, the Trust still retains the right to demand immediate transfer of the policy. The Trust may exercise this right by notifying the Company, in writing, within sixty (60) days after the Company Default occurs. Upon receipt of such notice, the Company shall immediately transfer ownership of the Policy to the Trust and the Company shall thereafter have no rights with respect to the Policy. The Trust's failure to exercise its rights under this Section 7 shall not be deemed to release the Company from any of its obligations under this Agreement, and shall not preclude the Trust from seeking other remedies with respect to the Company Default. For purposes of this Agreement, a "Company Default" shall be deemed to have occurred with respect to the Policy if the Company fails to pay a premium on the Policy as required under the terms of this Agreement within sixty (60) days after the due date for such premium, or if the Company processes or attempts to process a policy loan, or a complete or partial surrender, or a cash value withdrawal without prior written approval from the Trustees of the Trust. The Company shall notify the Trustees of the Trust within five (5) business days of any event which constitutes a Company Default.
perturbation.justification: The added sentence in the 'Changed Text' - 'However, even if the Company cures the default, the Trust still retains the right to demand immediate transfer of the policy' - directly contradicts the original intent of allowing the company to cure a default. The contradicted text assumes that curing the default would restore the company's rights, but the added sentence negates this, creating an irreconcilable inconsistency. This aligns with the 'Inconsistencies - In Text Contradiction' perturbation type, where different sections provide conflicting obligations. The cure provision is rendered meaningless, creating uncertainty about contractual obligations.
